name: Inner-City Muslim Action Network
mission: IMAN incorporated as a nonprofit in 1997 through the drive of people directly affected by and deeply invested in social issues affecting communities of color living on Chicago’s South Side. Since that time, IMAN has steadily grown and, in 2016, opened a second office in Atlanta to continue mobilizing a cross-section of people committed to this mission.
theoryOfChange: IMAN uses a holistic, integrative approach combining direct services (health, housing) with systemic change (organizing, advocacy) and cultural work (arts) to address structural injustices.
scoreSummary: INNER-CITY MUSLIM ACTION NETWORK shows good impact and good alignment, with zakat compliance (minor risk deductions applied).

evaluationSources: MacArthur Foundation Grant (to Executive Director Rami Nashashibi) (John D. and Catherine T. MacArthur Foundation)

zakatClaimEvidence: Dedicated zakat page found at https://imancentral.org/donate (Source: https://imancentral.org/donate)

all_citations.claim: IMAN provides transitional housing and construction training for returning citizens.
The organization is zakat-eligible and follows specific asnaf categories for distribution.
IMAN offers multiple donation options and giving methods for supporters.
IMAN engages the community through volunteer opportunities and grassroots organizing.
Financial data shows total revenue of $18,475,567, a program expense ratio of 65.9%, working capital of 9.9 months, and fundraising efficiency of $0.02 per $1 raised.

baseline_narrative.amal_score_rationale: IMAN demonstrates solid financial health and a direct-service impact model, though its program expense ratio suggests room for optimizing resource allocation toward direct programming.
areas_for_improvement: program expense ratio of 65.9% is lower than the industry benchmark for high-performing nonprofits
Limited public data regarding specific cost-per-beneficiary metrics

dimension_explanations.alignment: The organization is highly aligned with Muslim donor values as a ZAKAT-ELIGIBLE entity that addresses domestic poverty and recidivism [2]. Donors can contribute through various methods, including zakat-specific funds that support eligible community members in need [2][3].
dimension_explanations.impact: IMAN achieves a direct impact through its Green ReEntry program and community health centers, supported by a total revenue of $18,475,567 [1][5]. While the organization effectively manages fundraising costs at $0.02 per $1 raised, its program expense ratio stands at 65.9%, reflecting a balanced but moderate allocation toward direct services [5].

baseline_narrative.headline: The Inner-City Muslim Action Network (IMAN) fosters health, wellness, and healing in urban communities through integrated social services and reentry programs.
strengths: High fundraising efficiency with a cost of only $0.02 per $1 raised
Strong liquidity with 9.9 months of working capital available
Zakat-eligible programs that serve specific asnaf categories
baseline_narrative.summary: Operating in Chicago and Atlanta, IMAN provides transitional housing, life skills education, and sustainable construction training for formerly incarcerated individuals [1][5]. The organization maintains a holistic approach to community development by integrating a health center with grassroots organizing and the Green ReEntry program [4][5].

rich_narrative.headline: The Inner-City Muslim Action Network (IMAN) fosters health, wellness, and healing in urban communities through integrated social services and reentry programs.

ideal_donor_profile.best_for_summary: Best for donors who want to support holistic, domestic urban renewal and formerly incarcerated individuals through a faith-inspired framework.
donor_motivations: Want to fulfill zakat obligations within the United States
Value long-term community presence over rapid-response relief
Interested in the intersection of social justice and Islamic values
Support vocational training and sustainable 'green' development
giving_considerations: The organization operates in a niche space with limited large-scale peers
Recent revenue declines may indicate a transition period
ideal_donor_profile.not_ideal_for: Donors seeking high-volume international relief or those who prioritize charities with the highest possible program-to-overhead ratios.

impact_evidence.evidence_grade_explanation: While outcomes are verified, the lack of public beneficiary counts prevents a full cost-effectiveness evaluation.
impact_evidence.theory_of_change_summary: IMAN uses a 'Green ReEntry' model where vocational training in sustainable construction serves as a vehicle for both individual rehabilitation and neighborhood revitalization.
impact_evidence.why_evidence_matters: Clear data on how many individuals complete the program helps donors understand the scale of success per dollar spent.

long_term_outlook.room_for_funding_explanation: Despite significant assets, the recent decline in revenue suggests a need for new donor support to maintain program depth.

peer_comparison.differentiator: Unique integration of <cite id="4">sustainable construction training</cite> with <cite id="11">Islamic community values</cite>.

similar_organizations.differentiator: Focus on food security vs holistic reentry
similar_organizations.name: Atlanta Community Food Bank

strengths.detail: IMAN demonstrates high fiscal discipline in its development efforts, spending only <cite id="10">$0.03 to raise every $1</cite> in contributions.
The organization maintains <cite id="7">9.9 months of working capital</cite> in working capital, providing a significant buffer against economic volatility.
IMAN provides <cite id="11">zakat-eligible programs</cite> that specifically serve the <cite id="12">fuqara (poor) and masakin (needy)</cite> within urban American contexts.

strengths_deep_dive: IMAN's operational strength lies in its diverse revenue streams and community-embedded model. Beyond traditional donations, the organization generated <cite id="13">$2,979,023 in program service revenue</cite>, indicating a level of earned income through its health and vocational services. This is supported by a <cite id="14">100% independent board of directors</cite>, ensuring objective oversight of its <cite id="15">$17,786,361 in total assets</cite>. Their long-term presence since 1997 has allowed them to build deep trust in underserved neighborhoods, facilitating a <cite id="16">direct service delivery model</cite> that bypasses intermediaries to reach those most in need.
rich_narrative.summary: Founded in <cite id="1">1997</cite>, the Inner-City Muslim Action Network (IMAN) operates as a multifaceted community development organization focused on <cite id="2">Chicago and Atlanta</cite>. The organization addresses systemic poverty through an integrated model that combines <cite id="3">transitional housing, life skills education, and sustainable construction training</cite>. IMAN is particularly noted for its <cite id="4">Green ReEntry program</cite>, which provides formerly incarcerated individuals with vocational skills while improving the local housing stock. Financially, the organization managed <cite id="5">$15,923,414 in total revenue</cite> during the 2023 tax year, with a significant portion of its funding coming from <cite id="6">$12,509,163 in direct contributions</cite>. While the organization maintains <cite id="7">9.9 months of working capital</cite>, it has experienced a <cite id="8">3-year revenue CAGR of -21.1%</cite>, suggesting a period of financial consolidation or shifting grant cycles. IMAN distinguishes itself by bridging the gap between <cite id="9">grassroots organizing and direct social services</cite>, ensuring that community members are not just recipients of aid but active participants in local advocacy and arts-driven healing.
